hypothesis: If tensor completion surrogates (CPD-S and NeAT) trained on lattice design parameters can predict Young's modulus (E) with higher R^2 scores than baseline ML methods (Gaussian Process, XGBoost, and MLP) across both uniformly sampled and exponentially biased training data, then they provide a more accurate surrogate for lattice mechanical properties because they exploit the low-rank multi-way structure of the design–property tensor to improve generalization from limited or biased samples.
